content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 207 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1102 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

April 5, 1976

Mr. Stephen Reinhardt
Bodla, Fogel, Julber,
Reinhardt and Rothschild
5900 Wilshire Blvd., Suite 2600
Los Angeles, CA. 90036

Re: Request for Opinion
No. 76-035

Dear Mr. Reinhardt:

Thank you for your letter of March 17, 1976, requesting
an opinion with respect to the financial disclosure responsi-
bilities of designated employees under a Conflict of Inter-
est Code adopted pursuant to the Political Reform Act.
Since your letter raises no substantial questions involving
an interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be
issued. However, I hope the following informal comments
will be helpful.

Government Code Sections 67300, et seq. require each
state or local government agency to adopt a Conflict of
Interest Code. Any person who holds a position which en-
tails the making or participation in the making of govern-
mental decisions which may foreseeably have a material finan-
cial effect on any financial interest shall be a "designated
employee" under the Code. The Code must require that each
"designated employee" file statements disclosing reportable
financial interests. The Act identifies reportable finan-
cial interests generally as investments, interests in real
property and income.

As stated, income is among the items which may be made
reportable in an annual statement of financial interest of a
"designated employee." Under a Conflict of Interest Code,
income from sources which may foreseeably be affected mater-
ially by any decision made or participated in by the "desig-
nated employee" by virtue of his position must be reported
by the "employee." Gov. Code Section 87302(b). Income of
the "designated employee" includes his pro rata share of any
income of any business entity or trust in which the "employee"
or his or her spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially,
a ten percent interest or greater. Gov. Code Section 82030(a).

TECHNICAL ASSISTANCE/ADMINISTRATION (916) 322-5660 • EXECUTIVE/LEGAL 322-5901 • ENFORCEMENT/CONFLICT OF INTEREST 322-6141

APRIL 15, 1976
Page Two

The Act requires that, under a Conflict of Interest Code,
the manner of reporting reportable items shall be sub-
stantially equivalent to Article 2 of Chapter 7. Gov. Code
Section 87302(b). Thus,

"When income of a business entity, including income of
a sole proprietorship, is required to be reported ....,
the statement shall contain:

...In the case of a business entity which provides
legal,or brokerage services, the name of every person
who paid fees to the business entity if the filer's pro
rata share of fees from such person was equal to or
greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000) ..."

Gov. Code Section 87207(b)(2)

It is clear, therefore, that "designated employees"
need disclose clients of a law firm only where the desig-
nated employee or his or her spouse owns a ten percent in-
terest in the firm, the "employee's" pro rata share of the
fees paid by the client to the firm equalled $1,000 during
the reporting period, and there is reasonable foreseeability
that the client may be materially financially affected by a
governmental decision made or participated in by the desig-
nated official.

The determination of what kind of clients or clients'
interests may foreseeably be affected by a decision of the
"designated employee," like all other factual determinations
regarding a Conflict of Interest Code, resides in the first
instance with the Code adopting agency. The code reviewing
body is bound to reject or revise the determinations of the
agency only if the agency Code "[f]ails to provide reasonable
assurance that all foreseeable potential conflict of inter-
est situations will be disclosed or prevented." Gov. Code
Section 87309(a).

If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request,
you may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321.

I hope that the foregoing is helpful. If you have any
further questions, please contact Delbert Spurlock, Chief of
the Conflicts of Interest Division, (916) 322-6441.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Daniel H. Lowenstein
Chairman

DIL:pvp

Since your letter raises no substantial questions involving an interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. However, I hope the following informal comments will be helpful. Government Code Sections 87300, et seq., require each state or local government agency to adopt a Conflict of Interest Code. Any person who holds a position which entails the making or participation in the making of governmental decisions which may foreseeably have a material financial effect on any financial interest shall be a "designated employee" under the Code. The Code must require that each "designated employee" file statements disclosing reportable financial interests. The Act identifies reportable financial interests generally as investments, interests in real property and income. As stated, income is among the items which may be made reportable in an annual statement of financial interest of a "designated employee." Under a Conflict of Interest Code, income from sources which may foreseeably be affected materially by any decision made or participated in by the "designated employee" by virtue of his position must be reported by the "employee." Gov. Code Section 87302(b). Income of the "designated employee" includes his pro rata share of any income of any business entity or trust in which the "employee" or his or her spouse owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a ten percent interest or greater. Gov. Code Section 82030(a): The Act requires that, under a Conflict of Interest Code, the manner of reporting reportable items shall be substantially equivalent to Article 2 of Chapter 7. Gov. Code Section 87302(b). Thus, "When income of a business entity, including income of a sole proprietorship, is required to be reported ..., the statement shall contain: ... ...In the case of a business entity which provides legal or brokerage services, the name of every person who paid fees to the business entity if the filer's pro rata share of fees from such person was equal to or greater than one thousand dollars ($1,000) ..." Gov. Code Section 87207(b)(2) It is clear, therefore, that "designated employees" need disclose clients of a law firm only where the designated employee or his or her spouse owns a ten percent interest in the firm, the "employee's" pro rata share of the fees paid by the client to the firm equalled $1,000 during the reporting period, and there is reasonable foreseeability that the client may be materially financially affected by a governmental decision made or participated in by the designated official.
